title: Division by 2-digits
description: Divide 3-digit and 4-digit numbers by a 2-digit number.  Some quotients include remainders.
topic_path: Topics A, B, C, & D: Foundations

hints:
To divide larger numbers, we can split them into smaller numbers that we already know how to divide.

Let's think of a few multiples of $\maroonD{31}$ that may be useful:

- $\maroonD{31} \times 1 = 31$
- $\maroonD{31} \times 2 = 62$
- $\maroonD{31} \times 5 = 155$

$\maroonD{31} \times 20 $ is a little too large.  

So, let's start by dividing $589$ into $\purpleD{10}\maroonD{\text{ thirty-ones}}$.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{31}&\overline{\Big)589}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{310}}&\purpleD{10}&\maroonD{31} \times \purpleD{10} = \blueE{310}\\
&279\\
\end{array}$

We have $279$ left. That's a little less than $310$. So, let's try dividing $279$ into $9\maroonD{\text{ thirty-ones}}$.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{31}&\overline{\Big)589}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{310}&10\\
&279\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{279}}&\purpleD{9}&\maroonD{31} \times \purpleD{9} = \blueE{279}\\
&0\\
\end{array}$

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
{31}&\overline{\Big)589}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{310}&\purpleD{10}\\
&279\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{279}&\underline{{}+\purpleD{9}}\\
&\goldE{0}&\greenE{19}\\
\end{array}$

$\purpleD{10} + \purpleD{9}= \greenE{19}$, so our quotient is $\greenE{19}$, and we have no remainder.

$589 \div 31=\greenE{19}\text{ remainder }\goldE{0}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

To divide larger numbers, we can split them into smaller numbers that we already know how to divide.

Let's think of a few multiples of $\maroonD{20}$ that may be useful:

- $\maroonD{20} \times 2 = 40$
- $\maroonD{20} \times 3 = 60$
- $\maroonD{20} \times 4 = 80$

$\maroonD{20} \times 40 $ is a little too large.  

So, let's start by dividing $740$ into $\purpleD{30}\maroonD{\text{ twenties}}$.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{20}&\overline{\Big)740}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{600}}&\purpleD{30}&\maroonD{20} \times \purpleD{30} = \blueE{600}\\
&140\\
\end{array}$

We have $140$ left. We know that $7 \times 2=14$. So, let's try dividing $140$ into $7\maroonD{\text{ twenties}}$.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{20}&\overline{\Big)740}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{600}&30\\
&140\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{140}}&\purpleD{7}&\maroonD{20} \times \purpleD{7} = \blueE{140}\\
&0\\
\end{array}$

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
{20}&\overline{\Big)740}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{600}&\purpleD{30}\\
&140\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{140}&\underline{{}+\purpleD{7}}\\
&\goldE{0}&\greenE{37}\\
\end{array}$

$\purpleD{30} + \purpleD{7}= \greenE{37}$, so our quotient is $\greenE{37}$, and we have no remainder.

||
-: | :-
$\greenE{37}$ | remainder $\goldE{0}$
${20}\overline{\Big)740}$ |

[[☃ explanation 1]]

To divide larger numbers, we can split them into smaller numbers that we already know how to divide.

Let's think of a few multiples of $\maroonD{19}$ that may be useful:

- $\maroonD{19} \times 2 = 38$
- $\maroonD{19} \times 4 = 76$
- $\maroonD{19} \times 5 = 95$

$\maroonD{19} \times 40$ is close.  

So, let's start by dividing $798$ into $\purpleD{40}\maroonD{\text{ nineteens}}$.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{19}&\overline{\Big)798}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{760}}&\purpleD{40}&\maroonD{19} \times \purpleD{40} = \blueE{760}\\
&38\\
\end{array}$

We have $38$ left. We know that $19 \times 2=38$. So, let's try dividing $38$ into $2\maroonD{\text{ nineteens}}$.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{19}&\overline{\Big)798}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{760}&40\\
&38\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{38}}&\purpleD{2}&\maroonD{19} \times \purpleD{2} = \blueE{38}\\
&0\\
\end{array}$

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
{19}&\overline{\Big)798}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{760}&\purpleD{40}\\
&38\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{38}&\underline{{}+\purpleD{2}}\\
&\goldE{0}&\greenE{42}\\
\end{array}$

$\purpleD{40} + \purpleD{2}= \greenE{42}$, so our quotient is $\greenE{42}$, and we have no remainder.

$798 \div 19=\greenE{42}\text{ remainder }\goldE{0}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

To divide larger numbers, we can split them into smaller numbers that we already know how to divide.

Let's think of a few multiples of $\maroonD{96}$ that may be useful:

- $\maroonD{96} \times 2 = 192$
- $\maroonD{96} \times 3 = 288$
- $\maroonD{96} \times 5 = 480$

$\maroonD{96} \times 30$ is a little too large.  

So, let's start by dividing $2{,}688$ into $\purpleD{20}\maroonD{\text{ ninety-sixes}}$.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{96}&\overline{\Big)2{,}688}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{1{,}920}}&\purpleD{20}&\maroonD{96} \times \purpleD{20} = \blueE{1{,}920}\\
&768\\
\end{array}$

We have $768$ left. We know that $100\times 7=700$. So, let's try dividing $768$ into $7\maroonD{\text{ ninety-sixes}}$.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{96}&\overline{\Big)2{,}688}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{1{,}920}&20\\
&768\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{672}}&\purpleD{7}&\maroonD{96} \times \purpleD{7} = \blueE{672}\\
&96\\
\end{array}$

We have $96$ left. So we can divide it into $1$ more $\maroonD{\text{ninety-sixes}}$.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{96}&\overline{\Big)2{,}688}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{1{,}920}&20\\
&768\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{672}&7\\
&96\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{96}}&\purpleD{1}&\maroonD{96} \times \purpleD{1} = \blueE{96}\\
&0\\
\end{array}$

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
{96}&\overline{\Big)2{,}688}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{1{,}920}&\purpleD{20}\\
&768\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{672}&\purpleD{7}\\
&96\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{96}&\underline{{}+\purpleD{1}}\\
&\goldE{0}&\greenE{28}\\
\end{array}$

$\purpleD{20} + \purpleD{7} + \purpleD{1}= \greenE{28}$, so our quotient is $\greenE{28}$, and we have no remainder.

$2{,}688 \div 96=\greenE{28}\text{ remainder }\goldE{0}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

To divide larger numbers, we can split them into smaller numbers that we already know how to divide.

Let's think of a few multiples of $\maroonD{62}$ that may be useful:

- $\maroonD{62} \times 4 = 248$
- $\maroonD{62} \times 5 = 310$
- $\maroonD{62} \times 9 = 558$

$\maroonD{62} \times 90$ is a little too small.  

So, let's start by dividing $8{,}370$ into $\purpleD{100}\maroonD{\text{ sixty-twos}}$.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{62}&\overline{\Big)8{,}370}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{6{,}200}}&\purpleD{100}&\maroonD{62} \times \purpleD{100} = \blueE{6{,}200}\\
&2{,}170\\
\end{array}$

We have $2{,}170$ left. That's a little less than $2{,}480$. So, let's try dividing $2{,}170$ into $30\maroonD{\text{ sixty-twos}}$.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{62}&\overline{\Big)8{,}370}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{6{,}200}&100\\
&2{,}170\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{1{,}860}}&\purpleD{30}&\maroonD{62} \times \purpleD{30} = \blueE{1860}\\
&310\\
\end{array}$

We have $310$ left. So, let's try dividing $310$ into $5\maroonD{\text{ sixty-twos}}$.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{62}&\overline{\Big)8{,}370}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{6{,}200}&100\\
&2{,}170\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{1{,}860}&30\\
&310\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{310}}&\purpleD{5}&\maroonD{62} \times \purpleD{5} = \blueE{310}\\
&0\\
\end{array}$

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
{62}&\overline{\Big)8{,}370}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{6{,}200}&\purpleD{100}\\
&2{,}170\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{1{,}860}&\purpleD{30}\\
&310\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{310}&\underline{{}+\purpleD{5}}\\
&\goldE{0}&\greenE{135}\\
\end{array}$

$\purpleD{100} + \purpleD{30} + \purpleD{5}= \greenE{135}$, so our quotient is $\greenE{135}$, and we have no remainder.

$8{,}370 \div 62=\greenE{135}\text{ remainder }\goldE{0}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

To divide larger numbers, we can split them into smaller numbers that we already know how to divide.

Let's think of a few multiples of $\maroonD{20}$ that may be useful:

- $\maroonD{20} \times 2 = 40$
- $\maroonD{20} \times 3 = 60$
- $\maroonD{20} \times 5 = 100$

$\maroonD{20} \times 200 $ is too large.  

So, let's start by dividing $2{,}311$ into $\purpleD{100}\maroonD{\text{ twenties}}$.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{20}&\overline{\Big)2{,}311}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{2{,}000}}&\purpleD{100}&\maroonD{20} \times \purpleD{100} = \blueE{2{,}000}\\
&311\\
\end{array}$

We have $311$ left. That's a little less than $400$. So, let's try dividing $311$ into $10\maroonD{\text{ twenties}}$.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{20}&\overline{\Big)2{,}311}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{2{,}000}&100\\
&311\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{200}}&\purpleD{10}&\maroonD{20} \times \purpleD{10} = \blueE{200}\\
&111\\
\end{array}$

We have $111$ left. That's a little more than $100$. So, let's try dividing $111$ into $5\maroonD{\text{ twenties}}$.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{20}&\overline{\Big)2{,}311}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{2{,}000}&100\\
&311\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{200}&10\\
&111\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{100}}&\purpleD{5}&\maroonD{20} \times \purpleD{5} = \blueE{100}\\
&11\\
\end{array}$

We have $11$ left. That's less than $\maroonD{20}$, so we're done dividing.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
{20}&\overline{\Big)2{,}311}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{2{,}000}&\purpleD{100}\\
&311\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{200}&\purpleD{10}\\
&111\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{100}&\underline{{}+\purpleD{5}}\\
&\goldE{11}&\greenE{115}\\
\end{array}$

$\purpleD{100} + \purpleD{10} + \purpleD{5}= \greenE{115}$, so our quotient is $\greenE{115}$, and our remainder is $\goldE{11}$.

||
-: | :-
$\greenE{115}$ | remainder $\goldE{11}$
${20}\overline{\Big)2{,}311}$ |

[[☃ explanation 1]]

To divide larger numbers, we can split them into smaller numbers that we already know how to divide.

Let's think of a few multiples of $\maroonD{75}$ that may be useful:

- $\maroonD{75} \times 2 = 150$
- $\maroonD{75} \times 4 = 300$
- $\maroonD{75} \times 8 = 600$

$\maroonD{75} \times 40 $ is close.  

So, let's start by dividing $3{,}050$ into $\purpleD{40}\maroonD{\text{ seventy-fives}}$.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{75}&\overline{\Big)3{,}050}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{3{,}000}}&\purpleD{40}&\maroonD{75} \times \purpleD{40} = \blueE{3{,}000}\\
&50\\
\end{array}$

We have $50$ left. That's less than $\maroonD{75}$, so we're done dividing.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
{75}&\overline{\Big)3{,}050}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{3{,}000}&\underline{{}+\purpleD{40}}\\
&\goldE{50}&\greenE{40}\\
\end{array}$

So our quotient is $\greenE{40}$, and our remainder is $\goldE{50}$.

$3{,}050 \div 75=\greenE{40}\text{ remainder }\goldE{50}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

To divide larger numbers, we can split them into smaller numbers that we already know how to divide.

Let's think of a few multiples of $\maroonD{43}$ that may be useful:

- $\maroonD{43} \times 2 = 86$
- $\maroonD{43} \times 4 = 172$
- $\maroonD{43} \times 5 = 215$

$\maroonD{43} \times 200$ is too large.  

So, let's start by dividing $6{,}338$ into $\purpleD{100}\maroonD{\text{ forty-threes}}$.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{43}&\overline{\Big)6{,}338}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{4{,}300}}&\purpleD{100}&\maroonD{43} \times \purpleD{100} = \blueE{4{,}300}\\
&2{,}038\\
\end{array}$

We have $2{,}038$ left. That's a little less than $2{,}150$. So, let's try dividing $2{,}038$ into $40\maroonD{\text{ forty-threes}}$.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{43}&\overline{\Big)6{,}338}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{4{,}300}&100\\
&2{,}038\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{1{,}720}}&\purpleD{40}&\maroonD{43} \times \purpleD{40} = \blueE{1{,}720}\\
&318\\
\end{array}$

We have $318$ left. We know that $40\times 8=320$. So, let's try dividing $318$ into $7\maroonD{\text{ forty-threes}}$.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{43}&\overline{\Big)6{,}338}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{4{,}300}&100\\
&2{,}038\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{1{,}720}&40\\
&318\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{301}}&\purpleD{7}&\maroonD{43} \times \purpleD{7} = \blueE{301}\\
&17\\
\end{array}$

We have $17$ left. That's less than $\maroonD{43}$, so we're done dividing.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
{43}&\overline{\Big)6{,}338}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{4{,}300}&\purpleD{100}\\
&2{,}038\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{1{,}720}&\purpleD{40}\\
&318\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{301}&\underline{{}+\purpleD{7}}\\
&\goldE{17}&\greenE{147}\\
\end{array}$

$\purpleD{100} + \purpleD{40} + \purpleD{7}= \greenE{147}$, so our quotient is $\greenE{147}$, and our remainder is $\goldE{17}$.

$6{,}338 \div 43=\greenE{147}\text{ remainder }\goldE{17}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

To divide larger numbers, we can split them into smaller numbers that we already know how to divide.

Let's think of a few multiples of $\maroonD{31}$ that may be useful:

- $\maroonD{31} \times 2 = 62$
- $\maroonD{31} \times 3 = 93$
- $\maroonD{31} \times 4 = 124$

$\maroonD{31} \times 300 $ is a little too large.  

So, let's start by dividing $8{,}203$ into $\purpleD{200}\maroonD{\text{ thirty-ones}}$.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{31}&\overline{\Big)8{,}203}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{6{,}200}}&\purpleD{200}&\maroonD{31} \times \purpleD{200} = \blueE{6{,}200}\\
&2{,}003\\
\end{array}$

We have $2{,}003$ left. We know that $30 \times 60=1{,}800$. So, let's try dividing $2{,}003$ into $60\maroonD{\text{ thirty-ones}}$.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{31}&\overline{\Big)8{,}203}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{6{,}200}&200\\
&2{,}003\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{1{,}860}}&\purpleD{60}&\maroonD{31} \times \purpleD{60} = \blueE{1{,}860}\\
&143\\
\end{array}$

We have $143$ left. That's a little more than $124$. So, let's try dividing $143$ into $4\maroonD{\text{ thirty-ones}}$.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{31}&\overline{\Big)8{,}203}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{6{,}200}&200\\
&2{,}003\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{1{,}860}&60\\
&143\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{124}}&\purpleD{4}&\maroonD{31} \times \purpleD{4} = \blueE{124}\\
&19\\
\end{array}$

We have $19$ left. That's less than $\maroonD{31}$, so we're done dividing.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
{31}&\overline{\Big)8{,}203}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{6{,}200}&\purpleD{200}\\
&2{,}003\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{1{,}860}&\purpleD{60}\\
&143\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{124}&\underline{{}+\purpleD{4}}\\
&\goldE{19}&\greenE{264}\\
\end{array}$

$\purpleD{200} + \purpleD{60} + \purpleD{4}= \greenE{264}$, so our quotient is $\greenE{264}$, and our remainder is $\goldE{19}$.

||
-: | :-

[[☃ explanation 1]]

To divide larger numbers, we can split them into smaller numbers that we already know how to divide.

Let's think of a few multiples of $\maroonD{20}$ that may be useful:

- $\maroonD{20} \times 2 = 40$
- $\maroonD{20} \times 4 = 80$
- $\maroonD{20} \times 8 = 160$

$\maroonD{20} \times 400$ is close.  

So, let's start by dividing $9{,}492$ into $\purpleD{400}\maroonD{\text{ twenties}}$.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{20}&\overline{\Big)9{,}492}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{8{,}000}}&\purpleD{400}&\maroonD{20} \times \purpleD{400} = \blueE{8{,}000}\\
&1{,}492\\
\end{array}$

We have $1{,}492$ left. That's a little less than $1{,}600$. So, let's try dividing $1{,}492$ into $70\maroonD{\text{ twenties}}$.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{20}&\overline{\Big)9{,}492}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{8{,}000}&400\\
&1{,}492\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{1{,}400}}&\purpleD{70}&\maroonD{20} \times \purpleD{70} = \blueE{1{,}400}\\
&92\\
\end{array}$

We have $92$ left. That's a little more than $80$. So, let's try dividing $92$ into $4\maroonD{\text{ twenties}}$.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
\maroonD{20}&\overline{\Big)9{,}492}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{8{,}000}&400\\
&1{,}492\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{1{,}400}&70\\
&92\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{\blueE{80}}&\purpleD{4}&\maroonD{20} \times \purpleD{4} = \blueE{80}\\
&12\\
\end{array}$

We have $12$ left. That's less than $\maroonD{20}$, so we're done dividing.

$\begin{array}{rr|rl}
{20}&\overline{\Big)9{,}492}\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{8{,}000}&\purpleD{400}\\
&1{,}492\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{1{,}400}&\purpleD{70}\\
&92\\
&\mathllap{-}\underline{80}&\underline{{}+\purpleD{4}}\\
&\goldE{12}&\greenE{474}\\
\end{array}$

$\purpleD{400} + \purpleD{70} + \purpleD{4}= \greenE{474}$, so our quotient is $\greenE{474}$, and our remainder is $\goldE{12}$.

$9{,}492 \div 20=\greenE{474}\text{ remainder }\goldE{12}$
